name: Uranium
name_origin: Named after the planet Uranus, which had been discovered eight years before the element.

occurrence.abundance_summary: About 2.7 ppm in Earth's crust, more abundant than silver. Principal ores include uraninite (pitchblende), carnotite, autunite, and uranophane. Significant deposits occur in Kazakhstan, Canada, Australia, and Namibia.

compounds.name: Uranium dioxide
compounds.summary: Black ceramic solid used as the standard fuel in light-water nuclear reactors; pelletized and clad in zirconium alloy tubes.

compounds.name: Uranium hexafluoride
compounds.summary: Volatile white solid that sublimes at 56.5 degrees C; the only gaseous uranium compound at near-ambient conditions, used in gaseous diffusion and centrifuge enrichment of uranium isotopes.

compounds.name: Uranyl nitrate
compounds.summary: Yellow crystalline solid that fluoresces green; used in nuclear fuel reprocessing (PUREX process) and historically in photography and ceramics.

compounds.name: Triuranium octoxide (yellowcake)
compounds.summary: Olive-green to black solid; the most stable uranium oxide and the form in which uranium is typically traded as 'yellowcake' concentrate.

compounds.name: Uranium carbide
compounds.summary: Refractory ceramic studied as an advanced nuclear fuel for fast and high-temperature reactors.

uses: Fuel for nuclear power reactors (enriched in U-235)
Depleted uranium in armor-piercing ammunition and tank armor due to its high density
Radiation shielding in medical and industrial radiography
Coloring agent in historical uranium glass and ceramic glazes (vaseline glass)
reactivity_summary: A silvery-white metal that tarnishes in air to form a dark oxide layer. Powdered uranium is pyrophoric. It reacts with water (slowly when cold, rapidly when hot) and dissolves in acids. Chemistry is rich, dominated by the +4 and +6 oxidation states; the uranyl ion (UO2 2+) is particularly important.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Natural uranium consists primarily of U-238 (99.27 percent, half-life 4.468 billion years) and U-235 (0.72 percent, half-life 703.8 million years), with traces of U-234. U-235 and U-233 are fissile, while U-238 is fertile, capable of breeding to plutonium-239.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
